old_claim.note: Civic Contract Registries is contextual infrastructure or shared knowledge, not a strict hard prerequisite.

new_claim.note: The scoped technology is reusable written legal-document form; writing is the medium that carries the document structure and clauses.

source_shape.source_locator: Cambridge Core's book description states that legal documents written on papyrus are presented from Ptolemaic to late Byzantine Egypt with translations and expert commentary.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source frames the corpus as written papyrus legal documents from ancient Egypt, not registry entries alone.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The old edge made a civic registry the general prerequisite; the source pins this scoped legal-form corpus to written legal documents instead.

ontology_before: Standard Legal Forms depended on Civic Contract Registries, implying that public civic registration was a prerequisite for reusable legal-document templates.
ontology_after: Standard Legal Forms is scoped to written legal-document formats and clause patterns, with writing as the required medium and contract/public-archive practices as surrounding legal context.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: standard_legal_forms now requires writing and no longer directly depends on civic_contract_registries.
would_reject_if: A source shows this node is specifically about registry-issued standard forms rather than written papyrus or documentary legal forms.
The node is later widened back to all legal boilerplate across all societies and eras.
Civic contract registries become the only kept mechanism for formulaic legal documents.
short_reason: Reusable legal forms are a written-document and clause-pattern technology, not a civic registry dependency.
